subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 2: Is Matter Around Us Pure

key_points: Pure substances have fixed composition and distinct physical and chemical properties
Elements contain only one type of atom; compounds contain two or more elements chemically combined in fixed proportions
Mixtures can be separated by physical methods, while compounds require chemical methods for separation
Solutions, colloids, and suspensions differ in particle size, stability, and light scattering
Colloids show Brownian motion and Tyndall effect but do not settle on standing
Saturated, unsaturated, and supersaturated solutions depend on solute quantity relative to solubility at a given temperature
Separation techniques include: filtration, evaporation, distillation, fractional distillation, centrifugation, chromatography, and crystallization
Fractional distillation separates miscible liquids with close boiling points; chromatography separates solutes based on solubility and adsorption
examples: Distillation of water separates it from impurities or salts
Fractional distillation separates ethanol and water due to different boiling points
Chromatography separates pigments in ink based on solubility
Centrifugation separates cream from milk due to density differences
Crystallization purifies salts from impure solutions
formulas: Solubility = Mass of solute / Volume of solvent × 100
Semi-perimeter for Heron’s formula: s = (a+b+c)/2 (used for triangles, sometimes in solution problems in lab)
Saturated solution: solute present = maximum solubility at given temperature
exam_tips: Explain differences between elements, compounds, and mixtures with examples
Describe separation techniques clearly, including principle and example
Use Tyndall effect and Brownian motion to distinguish colloids from solutions and suspensions
Understand saturated, unsaturated, and supersaturated solutions numerically
Apply solubility formulas and separation methods in practical or numerical questions
